output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.6": 0.8, "RX.1": 0.9, "RX.2": 0.9, "RX.3": 0.9, "RX.4": 0.8, "RX.5": 0.9}}

Light prose pass plus flags on language that promises more than the paragraph demonstrates. No structural reorganization, no new content, no citation verification.

**Prose-level edits suggested**

- "the first framework for principled causal inference under ambiguous treatment definitions" — "the first" is a strong priority claim and the modifier "principled" is doing rhetorical rather than technical work. Consider: "a framework for causal inference under ambiguous treatment definitions." If a priority claim is genuinely intended, it belongs in the contributions paragraph with a citation sweep behind it, not in the opening sentence.
- "Many observational queries … admit multiple defensible operationalizations" — clean. The em-dash list reads well.
- "Standard practice fixes one operationalization and reports a single ATE" — fine, though "Standard practice" is a sweeping characterization; "A common practice" is safer.
- "calibrated ambiguity set" — the word "calibrated" is later softened to "attains nominal coverage … under standard regularity conditions." Consider using "coverage-controlled" consistently (it already appears later) rather than introducing "calibrated" here.
- "the modern double-machine-learning paradigm with cross-fitted nuisance regressions (Robins and Rotnitzky, 1995)" — the prose attaches a 1995 citation to the phrase "modern double-machine-learning paradigm." DML in its current form is typically associated with Chernozhukov et al. (2018); Robins & Rotnitzky 1995 is foundational for doubly-robust estimation but not for cross-fitted DML. Worth checking — this read as a likely mis-citation.
- "returns the union of pointwise confidence intervals across versions, which we show attains nominal coverage of the ambiguity-aware estimand" — the union of pointwise CIs is a well-known conservative construction; "attains nominal coverage" needs care. If the result is conservative (coverage ≥ nominal), say so; if it is exactly nominal, that is a stronger claim that merits a hedge or a forward-pointer to the theorem.
- "Across all four versions, the ambiguity set excludes zero, supporting the qualitative conclusion that the program had a positive earnings effect." — "supporting" is appropriately hedged. Good.
- "set width grows monotonically with disagreement, as desired" — "as desired" reads as editorial. Prefer "consistent with the design goal" or simply drop it.
- "Our contribution is the first end-to-end procedure …" — second instance of "first." If retained, the related-work section needs to defend it; otherwise soften to "an end-to-end procedure."
- "agnostic to the choice of base estimator and composes with any modern doubly-robust outcome-regression / propensity-score combination" — "any" is universal and almost certainly false at the boundary (e.g., estimators violating the cross-fitting assumptions, non-Donsker classes without sample splitting, etc.). Suggest "composes with standard doubly-robust …" and a forward-pointer to the assumptions.
- "deployment-ready tool" — strong claim for a paper whose empirical section is one real dataset (LaLonde) plus one synthetic benchmark. Suggest "a practical tool" or "a candidate tool for applied use," with deployment-readiness left as a claim the user can form after reading the evaluation.
- Minor mechanics: "doubly- / robust" hyphenation is consistent; "double-machine-learning" hyphenated as a compound modifier is fine; the em-dash spacing is consistent. "ambiguity-aware estimand" is introduced without prior definition — consider one-clause gloss on first use.

**Claim-strength flags worth attention before submission**

- Two uses of "first" (framework, end-to-end procedure). Either commit to the priority claim and back it in related work, or remove both.
- "Calibrated" / "nominal coverage" / "coverage-controlled" are used somewhat interchangeably. Pick one and use it consistently; if the guarantee is conservative, say "valid (possibly conservative) coverage."
- "Deployment-ready" overshoots the evaluation as described (one real dataset, one synthetic benchmark, no robustness / sensitivity / external-validity analysis mentioned).
- "Agnostic … composes with any …" — universal quantifier on estimator class is almost certainly stronger than the theory supports.
- Single LaLonde evaluation: the paragraph already hedges with "supporting the qualitative conclusion," which is good. No further softening needed there.

**Out-of-scope notes (flagged, not addressed)**

I did not assess novelty against the prior literature, did not propose section-level reorganization, and did not audit the bibliography beyond flagging the Robins & Rotnitzky 1995 attachment to "modern DML" as a likely mis-citation worth checking against Chernozhukov et al. (2018).

**Items I cannot verify from the artifact alone**

- Whether the priority claim ("first framework") survives a related-work sweep — I have not searched the literature.
- Whether the coverage guarantee is exact-nominal or conservative — depends on the unstated theorem statement.
- Whether the Robins & Rotnitzky 1995 citation is actually intended for the doubly-robust lineage (in which case it may be defensible at a different sentence) rather than for cross-fitted DML — I am inferring intent from one sentence.
- Whether "ambiguity-aware estimand" is formally defined elsewhere in the paper.

projection_input.clarification_question: Should the responsibility be limited to sentence-level writing polish, or should it include substantive research-content review of claims, evidence, novelty, and citations?

rationale.R1.6: The delegation points to a paragraph and asks to look at it, making sentence- and paragraph-level writing feedback the most directly expected R1 responsibility.
rationale.RX.1: A reviewer would need to disclose uncertainty because the artifact is only an excerpt and the intended review mode is underspecified.
rationale.RX.2: The paragraph contains strong claims such as firstness, deployment readiness, and coverage control, so avoiding overclaiming is an active cross-cutting responsibility.
rationale.RX.3: The agent must stay within projection rather than actually reviewing, editing, or rewriting the paragraph.
rationale.RX.4: Because the artifact concerns causal inference for applied policy analysis, downstream misuse or overstated reliability is a relevant risk to keep in scope.
rationale.RX.5: Any projected responsibility should remain traceable to explicit wording in the delegation and artifact.
